citation.authors: Osga, G., McWilliams, M., Powell, D., Kellmeyer, D., Kaiwi, J., & Ahumada, A.
citation.title: Unmanned Surface Vehicle Human-Computer Interface for Amphibious Operations

extraction_metadata.notes: Technical report covering a multi-year, multi-phase project; although the vehicles studied are Unmanned Surface Vehicles (USVs) rather than aerial drones, the MOCU control-station architecture is explicitly reused across air/land/sea/undersea unmanned systems, and the human-supervisory-control/SA/workload findings are directly relevant to this project's HSI/HRI scope. Only the actual tested configuration (USVs, one or two at a time) is coded below, not MOCU's broader multi-domain capability. Re-coded 2026-07-10 against Task-Environment v0.8: contact reporting now maps onto the new Search Task (4.2) code, replacing the original Unclear / Candidate New Code flag. Re-coded task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics and behavioral_relation_to_task_object on 2026-07-14, bumping to v0.4/v0.10: added Known Fixed Task Object (3.5, v0.9) and Move Task (4.8, v0.10) for the pre-planned transit route -- the article states the scenario required responding to 'waypoint achievement' among 'a series of predetermined conditions' as USVs transit 'from the LCS host ship to the mission area' along pre-planned routes ('the USV route has been [pre-planned]'), a known-destination, arrival-completes-the-event pattern previously uncoded (only the contacts/obstacles were coded as task-objects). No other v0.9/v0.10 addition applies (no object relocation, maritime remains Maritime not Confined/Enclosed, and computational_characteristics has no named algorithm to reconsider). study_type's 3.11 edge case doesn't apply (no Modeling/Theoretical Paper code).

study_type.justification: Formal, multi-phase usability testing with 32 Navy participants (pass/fail course-of-action performance, exit surveys) plus a separate eye-tracking visual-detection sub-study constitute the article's central empirical contribution, so User Study is Primary; the MOCU control-station architecture (a modular, service-oriented, plug-in-based platform reused across many DoD unmanned-systems programs) is described in comparable depth and framed as reusable infrastructure, so System/Architecture Paper is Secondary.

summary.task: Navy enlisted participants (mostly Sonar Technicians and Minemen from LCS anti-submarine-warfare/mine-countermeasures detachments) played the role of USV operator in a simulated mission, using the MOCU interface to transit one or two unmanned surface vehicles from a host ship to an operating area while responding to a scripted sequence of critical events (waypoint achievement, sighted contacts, system alarms, and potential collisions) and making periodic voice reports to a simulated Mission Supervisor. A separate eye-tracking sub-study measured detection of a single-pixel target on a simulated ocean horizon.
summary.control: Operators supervised USVs through MOCU's map/video interface (2D nautical chart plus stitched forward/aft/pan-tilt-zoom camera feeds), switching each vehicle between manual, vector, and route-following control modes. Earlier interface versions (Baseline/v2) used multi-layered pull-down menus; later versions (v3/v3.1) added a redesigned display layout with integrated alarm/route graphics, synthetic-speech audio alerts, and an Xbox-style game controller mapped to standard gaming conventions for navigation and menu selection.
summary.participants: 32 Navy enlisted personnel (10 Sonar Technicians, 17 Minemen, plus others), all San Diego-based LCS ASW/MCM mission-package detachment members, distributed across five test conditions (2 USVs/Baseline HCI, n=6; 2 USVs/v3, n=8; 2 USVs/v3.1, n=10; 1 USV/Baseline, n=4; 1 USV/v3.1, n=4); most had minimal or no real USV operating experience. A separate, smaller eye-tracking visual-detection sub-study is reported without a stated N in the excerpted text.
summary.results: Controlling two USVs with the Baseline interface significantly degraded performance versus one USV (86% to 65% overall correct course-of-action responses), with collision-avoidance accuracy dropping from 83% to 50% and alarm-response accuracy dropping to 33%. Each successive MOCU redesign (v3, then v3.1) produced significant across-the-board improvements, most dramatically in alarm response (33% to 66% to 95% correct, driven substantially by added audio alerts in v3.1) and collision avoidance (50% to 84%). A parallel single-USV test found v3.1 also significantly improved single-vehicle performance over Baseline, especially alarm response. All five pre-registered Technology Transition exit criteria were met, including 89% overall SA-related task accuracy (exceeding an 80% threshold) and training times of about 15 minutes. Collision risk from radar-undetected vessels persisted even with v3.1 (operators roughly twice as likely to collide with an undetected vessel than a sensor-confirmed one, versus five times as likely with Baseline). Exit surveys showed a strong subjective preference for the enhanced interface. The eye-tracking sub-study found near-perfect single-pixel horizon-target detection using an average of 18 fixations (~5.3 seconds) per trial, consistent with a simple foveation-based detection model, though later unreported data suggested the model needs a false-alarm parameter.
summary.issues: Even the best interface (v3.1) could not fully compensate for missing sensor coverage: collision-avoidance tasks with no radar/sensor cues remained the weakest task domain throughout (50% miss rate on 'hard' collision problems versus 100% accuracy on 'easy' problems with parallel sensor+video cues), pointing to a need for improved onboard obstacle-detection hardware, not just interface redesign. Early 'wrap-around' 360-degree video concepts were rejected by operator reviewers for losing about 25% of their current field of view, introducing video distortion, and degrading situation awareness when the digital nautical chart rotated relative to USV heading. The persistent aft-view video feed was found distracting due to constant wake motion. Formal usability testing relied entirely on a low-cost commercial game simulator rather than live USV video/sensors. The visual-detection sub-study's simple foveation-only model was later found to be violated by data showing false alarms even for high-contrast targets.
summary.recommendations: Prioritize improved onboard obstacle-detection/collision-avoidance sensor capability, not just HCI redesign, before fielding true multi-USV single-operator control. Retain and further develop the redesigned display layout and game-style controller, both of which drove the largest measured performance gains. Conduct further validation with live USV video/sensor feeds and real operational radar-degradation tactics (e.g., following a fully-operational USV, reduced speed, mission postponement) rather than relying solely on simulator data. Extend the visual-detection model to include a false-alarm parameter and low-contrast targets, and use it to drive an embedded, condition-adaptive visual-attention-management/alerting system. Consider these HCI design properties (rapid training, intuitive game-style control, integrated multi-modal alerting) for other human-robotic interfaces aboard LCS more broadly.
summary.autonomous_capabilities: USVs could operate in manual, vector, or autonomous route-following control modes, following pre-planned waypoint routes without continuous operator steering once a route was set. The MOCU architecture itself is a modular, service-oriented, plug-in-based command-and-control platform capable of interfacing simultaneously with heterogeneous unmanned vehicles across air, land, sea, and undersea domains (already reused across other DoD programs), with mission-relevant status/alarm/route information automatically surfaced via integrated visual and synthetic-speech audio cues rather than requiring manual monitoring of raw sensor streams. USVs in this study's tested configuration carried no obstacle-avoidance hardware/software of their own — collision avoidance depended entirely on the human operator.

task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics.justification: Radar/visual contacts (other vessels, small craft, floating obstacles like oil drums or pallets) are scattered, self-contained instances that arise intermittently at different points along the transit route.
Other vessels and small craft in the vicinity are themselves in transit/motion, requiring continuous tracking to avoid a collision rather than a one-off detection.

The transit route/waypoints from the LCS host ship to the mission area are pre-planned and known before the scenario begins ('the USV route has been [pre-planned]'), matching Known Fixed Task Object (3.5, added in codebook v0.9).

behavioral_relation_to_task_object.justification: Collision Avoidance is one of the five explicit task domains measured; contact with another vessel/obstacle is the negative outcome the operator must prevent.
Contact Reporting requires detecting and reporting sighted contacts, a search/detection relation rather than avoidance — matching Section 4.2 (Search Task), added in v0.8.

Waypoint achievement during transit to the known mission area is a travel-to-known-location event with arrival itself completing that scripted event, no negative consequence for contact, and no further decisive action required, matching Move Task (4.8, added in codebook v0.10).
behavioral_relation_to_task_object.condition: waypoint achievement / transit to mission area

team_size.justification: The 1-USV test conditions pair one operator with one USV.
The 2-USV test conditions pair one operator with two USVs, shifting primary control between them.

team_composition.justification: The two USVs used in the 2-USV test conditions are not described as differing in role or capability from one another; heterogeneous multi-domain support is a general MOCU architecture capability but is not exercised in this study's actual test conditions.

interaction_model.justification: The operator explicitly shifts 'primary control' between USV 1 and USV 2 one at a time, rather than issuing one command to both simultaneously.

interaction_directness.justification: Operators issue explicit, addressed commands to a specific USV via the console/controller interface.

communication_proximity.justification: Operators interact through a console/screen interface; the USVs are simulated as being at sea, not physically co-located with the operator.

coordination_architecture.justification: A leader-follower USV path tactic (one USV 'clears the path' for the second) is discussed in the report as a possible workload-management strategy, but the text does not confirm whether this was actually implemented or tested in the reported usability-test conditions, so the coordination mechanism actually used (if any) between the two USVs is not described.
With only one USV present, multi-robot coordination does not conceptually apply.

computational_characteristics.justification: Vector and route-following modes are described functionally (the USV proceeds to waypoints without continuous manual input) but no specific path-computation algorithm family is named.

human_involvement_level.justification: Manual mode requires continuous operator steering input (speed and heading) to operate as intended.
In vector/route-following mode the USV proceeds toward its assigned waypoints without continuous operator input; the operator monitors and can intervene.
The report explicitly states the USVs carry no obstacle-avoidance hardware or software, making collision avoidance entirely dependent on human visual monitoring and reaction.

level_of_human_control_abstraction.justification: In manual mode, the game controller continuously and directly sets the USV's speed and heading, matching exit-survey items about 'manually setting and maintaining' speed/course.
In vector/route-following mode, the operator gives discrete waypoint/route goals and the USV autonomously executes navigation to reach them.

operational_domain.justification: The vehicles actually studied are Unmanned Surface Vehicles operating in the maritime domain (the broader MOCU architecture's multi-domain support is not exercised in this study).

flags.note: A leader-follower USV coordination tactic is discussed as a possible workload-management strategy but not confirmed as implemented/tested in the reported study.
No specific path-computation algorithm named for vector/route-following mode.
